Introduction:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The story of Artificial Intelligence (AI) is humanity’s relentless pursuit to build machines that can think, learn, and act. It is a journey marked by theoretical brilliance, seasons of doubt, and revolutionary breakthroughs that have fundamentally reshaped technology and society.
The foundation was laid in the 1930s-1950s by pioneers like Alan Turing, whose work provided the blueprint for computing and introduced the Turing Test as a benchmark for machine intelligence. The field was formally born at the 1956 Dartmouth Workshop, instilling early optimism with foundational concepts like the Perceptron and basic chatbots such as ELIZA.
However, the difficulty of real-world problems led to the disappointing “AI Winters,” where funding and progress stalled. The field was reignited by the practical success of Expert Systems and the refinement of the Backpropagation algorithm, which enabled neural networks to learn more effectively.
The modern AI era was decisively launched in 2012 with the victory of AlexNet in the ImageNet Challenge, powerfully demonstrating the potential of Deep Learning and the hardware that powered it (GPUs). This led to rapid advances, including the mastery of complex games by AlphaGo and the invention of the Transformer architecture, which is the core innovation behind today’s largest models.
The release of ChatGPT in 2022 pushed Generative AI — the ability of machines to create text, images, and code — into the mainstream.
Today, the frontier lies in Agentic AI — systems capable of autonomous reasoning, planning, and decision-making. The evolution of AI has taken machines from theoretical concepts to passive tools, and now, to active, intelligent collaborators that are poised to define the future of work and discovery.
The AI field has experienced an explosive growth this year, driven by a powerful synergy between two key factors: enhanced GPU technology and the ever-increasing availability of data (Big Data). Modern Graphics Processing Units (GPUs), with their architecture designed for massive parallel processing, have become the indispensable workhorses for training complex deep learning models, drastically cutting down the time required for computation. Concurrently, the proliferation of digital data provides the necessary ‘fuel’ for these powerful algorithms. The combination of significantly faster compute power and larger, more diverse datasets is what enables the creation of increasingly sophisticated and capable AI systems, leading to breakthroughs across areas like generative AI, large language models, and real-time decision-making applications.
Conceptual Foundation Of AI
Alan Tuning
Alan Mathison Turing (1912–1954) was one of the greatest thinkers of the 20th century. He is often called the father of modern computing because his ideas shaped how computers work today. Turing was not only a mathematician but also a codebreaker, a pioneer in artificial intelligence, and even a researcher in biology. His work has influenced many fields and continues to guide technology and science in the modern world.
In 1936, Turing introduced the idea of the Turing machine, a simple but powerful model that explained how a machine could follow rules to solve problems. This concept showed what it means for something to be “computable” and helped create the idea of the Universal Turing Machine — a machine that can solve any problem if given the right instructions. This idea is the foundation of every computer we use today, from laptops to smartphones.
Turing also played a key role in World War II at Bletchley Park, the British codebreaking center. He designed a machine called the Bombe, which helped break the German Enigma code. This allowed the Allies to read secret German messages, which many historians say shortened the war by years and saved millions of lives.
After the war, Turing worked on building real computers, including the design of the Automatic Computing Engine (ACE). He was also one of the first to ask whether machines could “think.” In 1950, he proposed the famous Turing Test, a method for determining whether a machine can exhibit human-like intelligence. This became the starting point for the field of artificial intelligence.
In his later years, Turing turned to biology. In 1952, he published a paper on morphogenesis, which explained how patterns in nature, like stripes on animals or spirals in plants, can form through simple chemical processes. This work is still important in biology today.
Sadly, despite his brilliant mind, Turing’s life ended in tragedy. In 1952, he was punished for being homosexual, which was illegal in Britain at the time. He was forced to undergo chemical treatment, and in 1954, he died under mysterious circumstances, officially recorded as suicide. It was only many years later that the world recognized his contributions. In 2013, he was granted a royal pardon, and today he is remembered as both a scientific genius and a symbol of courage.
Alan Turing’s story is one of intelligence, creativity, and resilience. His ideas made modern computing possible, his codebreaking helped save millions of lives, and his vision of thinking machines continues to inspire the future of artificial intelligence.
Tunning Machine:
The Turing machine is a theoretical model of computation that serves as the foundation of modern computer science. It was conceived by Alan Turing in 1936 to formally define the concept of an algorithm and to explore the limits of what machines can compute.
In simple terms, a Turing machine is an abstract device capable of performing any calculation that a human or real-world computer can. It operates using a tape divided into cells, each holding a symbol (commonly 0 or 1), a head that reads and writes symbols, and a set of rules that dictate its actions.
When the operation starts, the machine manipulates the symbols on the tape according to the instructions provided — writing 0 or 1. These instructions are often encoded in a Tibo card, which functions as the “program” for the machine, telling it how to move the head, which symbol to write, and what state to transition into next. Once the machine reaches the halt state, the sequence of 0s and 1s on the tape represents the output or result of the computation based on the input.
This concept illustrates that a Turing machine can, in theory, calculate anything that is computationally possible, given the correct set of instructions.
Components used
- Tape (Memory): An infinitely long strip of tape divided into individual cells. Each cell can hold a single symbol (usually a ‘0’ or ‘1’ or a blank symbol). This represents the machine’s external memory.
- Head (Processor): A device that sits over one cell of the tape at a time. It can read the symbol in that cell, write a new symbol to that cell, and move one cell to the left or right.
- State Register (Internal State): A component that records the machine’s current “state” from a finite set of possible states. The state determines what the machine does next.
Table of Instructions (Program): A finite set of rules (the “program”) that dictate the machine’s actions. A typical instruction might be:
- “If the machine is in State A and the head reads a ‘0’, then write a ‘1’, move right, and transition to State B.”
Tibo card
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This card is a Turing Machine Instruction Card, often called a Tibo Card in the context of this video, which features a model of a Turing machine designed by Ros Tibo for the Busy Beaver game.
The card acts as the machine’s “program,” defining the actions it must take based on what it reads on the tape and where it should move next. The specific instructions that control the machine’s left and right movement are an integral part of each rule on the card.
Based on the instructions on your “Card 1” image and the key explained in the video:
The Direction of Movement
In this binary Turing machine model, the movement of the read/write head is coded as a single digit on the instruction card, following the rule [[04:27](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DILF8usqp7M&t=267)]:
- 0 = Move Left (L)
- 1 = Move Right (R)
Instructions on Card 1
“Card 1” contains a set of two instructions — one for when the machine reads a ‘0’, and one for when it reads a ‘1’. These are the actions the machine takes while it is in “State 1” (i.e., operating under Card 1).
How it Works
The machine operates in discrete steps:
1. It observes its current state and the symbol under its head.
2. Based on the pre-defined instruction table, it performs the following actions:
- Writes a new symbol to the cell (or keeps the old one).
- Moves the head one cell left or right.
- Transitions to a new state.
3. The process repeats until the machine reaches a designated halt state.
Significance: The Universal Turing Machine (UTM)
Turing’s most profound insight was the concept of the Universal Turing Machine (UTM) .
- A UTM is a single, special Turing machine that can read the description (the instruction table) of any other Turing machine and then perfectly simulate that machine’s computation.
- This proved that one single machine, by simply changing its input program, could perform any possible algorithm or computation.
- This theoretical discovery is the direct foundation of the modern digital computer. It shows that the computer you use today doesn’t need to be redesigned for every new task (word processing, video editing, gaming); it just needs a different set of instructions (a program or software
The Church-Turing Thesis
The power of the Turing machine is formalized by the Church-Turing Thesis, a fundamental concept in computer science. It states that:
Any function that can be computed by an algorithm can be computed by a Turing machine.
In essence, if a problem can be solved by any sequence of mechanical steps, then a Turing machine can solve it. This thesis sets the theoretical boundary for all of computer science: if a Turing machine cannot solve a problem (like the Halting Problem, which Turing also proved), then no computer can solve it.
The Turing machine is a theoretical mathematical model of computation developed by Alan Turing in 1936. It is universally regarded as the foundational concept of modern computer science and the central processing unit (CPU).
Despite its extreme simplicity, it’s capable of simulating any computer algorithm and sets the theoretical limits of what is computable.
Early AI & The First Winter (1957–1986)
Frank Rosenblatt
Frank Rosenblatt (1928–1971) was an American psychologist and computer scientist, widely regarded as the father of the perceptron, one of the earliest models of artificial neural networks. Born into a Jewish family in New Rochelle, New York, he was the son of Dr. Frank and Katherine Rosenblatt. After graduating from The Bronx High School of Science in 1946, he studied at Cornell University, earning his A.B. in 1950 and Ph.D. in 1956. For his doctoral work, Rosenblatt built the Electronic Profile Analyzing Computer (EPAC), a custom machine designed to process complex psychometric data — an early demonstration of his innovative approach to combining psychology and computation.
Rosenblatt’s career advanced at the Cornell Aeronautical Laboratory in Buffalo, where he rose to become head of the Cognitive Systems Section. It was here that he began his groundbreaking research on neural networks, culminating in the creation of the Mark I Perceptron in 1960. This device, often described as the first computer that could “learn” through trial and error, attracted worldwide attention and was hailed as a revolution in computing. In 1962, he published *Principles of Neurodynamics*, which detailed his theories of perceptrons and brain mechanisms, solidifying his reputation as a leading figure in AI.
